U.S. refined petroleum stocks slump at fastest rate on record

U.S. refined oil stocks are falling at the fastest rate on record for the time of year as refiners prove unable to meet strong export demand stemming from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz while maintaining supply to domestic customers.

Combined stocks of gasoline, diesel, jet fuel and other refined products at refineries, tank farms and in pipelines have depleted by 45 million barrels since the war between the United States and Iran started at the end of February.

During the previous ten years, stocks depleted by an average of just 2 million barrels over the same period, and never by more than 25 million, according to weekly data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

U.S. DISTILLATE STOCKS have fallen to the lowest for the time of year for more two decades as closure of the Strait of Hormuz creates a worldwide shortage of diesel and similar fuels. Since the war began, distillate inventories have depleted by more than 18 million barrels, the largest seasonal draw down since 2018, and well above the average draw of 9 million barrels over the last ten years. Inventories were 25 million barrels (-19% or -1.33 standard deviations) below the seasonal average on May 1 up from a deficit of 15 million barrels (-11% or -0.91σ) immediately before the war started:

The US has suddenly activated three E-11A airborne communication relay aircraft — the so-called “flying Wi-Fi” for special forces — near Iranian borders. One is operating over Iraq, one over the Persian Gulf, and one over Saudi Arabia. This is the first time these platforms have been seen in action since the escalation began.

The flights coincide with other US reconnaissance assets, including an RQ-4B Global Hawk and a P-8A Poseidon, while American special forces remain positioned in Kuwait. The timing is notable given Trump’s recent statements about seeking peace.

SHELL WARNS OF 1 BILLION-BARREL CRUDE SUPPLY GAP

Shell says the global oil market is facing a growing supply shortage of nearly 1 billion barrels, with the gap widening daily.

CEO Wael Sawan said tight supply is driving high prices and weakening refining margins, while CFO Sinead Gorman confirmed excess cash will be used for share buybacks.

The company also flagged early signs of demand destruction in jet fuel and rising strain on refining, though upstream and gas operations are expected to offset some pressure.

US TO SANCTION IRAQI OFFICIAL OVER IRANIAN OIL SALES 

The U.S. Treasury plans to sanction Iraq’s deputy oil minister, Ali Maarij Al-Bahadly, accusing him of helping facilitate Iranian oil sales in violation of sanctions.

Officials allege he authorized large-scale diversion of Iraqi crude and helped disguise it as Iraqi oil by blending it with Iranian supply, enabling smuggling through regional networks.

The move marks a rare sanction against a sitting senior Iraqi official and signals increased U.S. pressure on Baghdad over its economic ties to Tehran.

Oil market liquidity has evaporated since the start of the US-Iran war, amplifying price moves in a benchmark seen as a bellwether for the global economy.

Total Brent futures open interest, a proxy for money in the market, plunged to the lowest level since August as traders increasingly shy away from the volatility tied to the Middle East conflict. The shrinking participation comes as the longest blockade of the Strait of Hormuz on record cuts off hundreds of millions of barrels of Persian Gulf oil from global markets, sparking an energy crisis.

The US gas exporter cited “unfavorable variances related to changes in the fair value of our derivative instruments” for the first-quarter loss, according to a statement Thursday. All 14 analysts in a Bloomberg survey had been expecting Cheniere to post positive net income.

Imagine you are the Iranian leadership reading this.

First, they’ll think Trump is being consistent. Iran has constantly emphasized its sincerity regarding negotiations while criticizing Trump’s rhetoric.

Now he is trying to force them back to the table again, threatening to suppress them with even more powerful and violent methods. Naturally this seems far from sincere.

But if they read it again, a different thought will emerge: "This guy is so weak that he’s practically begging for a deal even after his warships were attacked. He’s clearly desperate and terrified of restarting the war."

So what should they do? They’ll keep the situation in a deadlock and exploit his impatience and fear to extract as many concessions as possible. This is exactly what Iran is doing right now.

I’m not going to argue whether restarting the war is right or wrong. However to Iran, Trump is a pushover. No matter how much the MAGA crowd protests, that’s an undeniable fact.

He is undermining his own bargaining power far too easily. He might think he looks strong(as do his few remaining supporters), but his recent remarks and posts are nothing but begging for a deal "despite everything."

Iran has truly caught themselves a sucker. They will keep the situation stalled and domesticate Trump using a carrot and stick approach—until he either becomes a lame duck or makes an absurd level of concessions.

Of course the scope of those concessions will be so pathetic that even Trump won’t be able to spin it, which will ultimately deal a fatal blow to his political career.

Iran will continue to feed Trump the vain hope of a negotiation. Of course it will never be more than just hope. Meanwhile, time is just slipping away.

#oott #iran

text:
Thanks for the great point. I also appreciate the insightful comments from @ALikhodedov , @OilCfd , and other traders on my original post.

To dive a bit deeper, I’m not saying that the scale of supply loss is exaggerated or the underlying facts of the oil market have changed. 

The loss from the MEG is still massive, and Hormuz remains closed.

However we need to look at it from the physical buyers' perspective. Let me quote a bit from the report I wrote last week:

"Asia has gained some breathing room as panic-bought cargoes from the early stages of the conflict are now arriving, and they are betting and praying for a quick resolution.

They believe Iran’s resolve and leverage have weakened, and the fact China is releasing barrels from its SPR has given them hope.

For the next week or two, this hope and wait-and-see approach may prevail, likely keeping prices within a large range with a set ceiling. We might even touch the floor first."

Plz don't misunderstand—I'm not trying to claim victory here. I also didn't expect this barrage of news, so I bought in a bit too early yesterday and took a loss.

With barrels and freight being so expensive, no one wants to risk being the fool who bid at an outrageous price if a deal actually goes through. Of course they can't stay on the sidelines forever.

But from a timing perspective, just as we’ve gained enough physical room to reassess the situation, news headlines are injecting hope into the market.

If the physical need were desperate, they would be forced to bid regardless of that hope. But the timing of the May arrivals in Asia coincides perfectly with the revival of this optimism, causing benchmarks to shake.

The question shifts from "Where do I get barrels right now?" to "If the Strait opens, won't we be facing an oversupply of physical barrels?"

Consequently, until the buying cycle resumes, buyers have the luxury to wait instead of immediately snapping up additional cargoes from WAF, Russia, Latam. This in turn, keeps physical diffs suppressed.

I believe we should approach this from a psychological standpoint. Most Asian buyers I’ve spoken with here share this sentiment.

If the facts don't change, this won't last long. Whether they run out of feedstock or their hopes are crushed, they will eventually have to move.

Until then, there's no need to be impatient. Just watch if the facts change and let time do its work. We can't control the process anyway.

#oott #iran

quotedTweet.text: This is why I'm watching the May WAF programme. It's been slow so far given this wait and see attitude. Asia is sleepwalking into needing the most prompt barrel when the peace deal breaks down (WAF for Asia ex. PG). If that starts to move, the rest of the market will follow

text:
+ Aside from my criticism of fake news, vol is only natural. I finished March and April with great results, but it wasn't an easy ride. Of course i could be wrong this time, but I’m fine since I’m sitting on a thick profit buffer.

That said i believe you have to give up on trying to control the process. You have to position yourself accordingly. Given the oil vol since March, controlling the process is impossible. I think it’s better to just bet on the outcome.

That’s why I don’t really understand the oil traders who are feeling resentful or discouraged by this vol right now. 

Based on the price action over the last two months, didn't everyone know it would be like this? It's a market where controlling the process is virtually impossible for both buyers and sellers.

Wash, rinse, repeat. I think that old saying is in full effect. We also shouldn't overlook the very high probability these price swings will impact real-world outcomes.

Thank you.
